paragraphs.text: Truth is very much stranger than fiction, and
, belonging to a class in society far different to that adorned by an English young lady of fashion, has recently been really discovered on the island of
, where she appears to have spent the last eighteen years of her life.
—we are sorry to have to speak of her in the past tense, but she is no more—was, according to a San Francisco newspaper, an Indian woman, and her existence was accidentally discovered by
to hunt for otters, and who found human footprints deeply imbedded in the soil. But he soon lost the track, and during three years' periodical excursions to
he was unable to light upon any further signs indicating the whereabouts of the "human." At length
one of the party who accompanied him
, who was found in a kind of kraal or circular enclosure of brushwood, about five feet high and six feet in diameter, with a small lateral opening.
was attired in a garment made from the skin of the "shag," a species of an ugly and unwieldy duck that can neither walk nor fly.
It was a most clumsy costume, and reached nearly to her ancles [ankles] when she stood erect. When "interviewed" by

she was sitting cross-legged, engaged in the useful but slightly malodorous occupation of skinning seal blubber with a rude knife fashioned from a piece of iron hoop driven into a piece of wood. She had no head-gear beyond a great shock of matted hair, which, probably owing to its constant exposure to the sun and the weather, had assumed a yellowish-brown hue, and which was short, as though the ends had rotted away.
Now, Indians are almost always black-haired, and the comal phenomena of
suggests a cheap and efficacious recipe for changing a brunette into a blonde, and shortening the hair without cutting it.
Only the patient will have to go about bareheaded until the beneficent sun turns the dark locks, auburn and singes the ends.
There were some half-wild dogs on
, and a few of these animals kept near the kraal of
, and seemed to regard her as
As for her diet she appears to have lived chiefly on the cabbage-like plant known to the Californians as "palo santo," also on a root called "corcomite."
This will be pleasant news for the vegetarians; but the gratification of
will be lessened when it is added that
was much addicted to the consumption of seal blubber.

She was also found to be in the possession of a rude apparatus for catching shell fish, and her stock of string fishing lines, made from the sinews of seals, seemed to point to her having occasionally pursued deep sea fishing.

Her countenance is described as pleasing, her features as regular, her complexion fairer, and her form more symmetrical than those of the Indian women on the mainland. Perhaps she was not an Indian after all; but a Saratoga belle who had repented of her watering place levities, and taken refuge in this solitude, where she had remained until all faculty of articulate speech had departed from her.
It must be remembered that, unlike the original Robinson, she had no parrot, and consequently no facilities for conversational practice.
Civilization is but skin deep; and it is astonishing in how brief a time Man, left to himself, will begin to run about on all fours and bark.
The female Crusoe of San Nicholas
did not bark, it is true; but she did not seem to understand any of the Indian dialects spoken in
, and did not volunteer any gibberish of her own.
She was a a [sic] kind of Californian
, for, although substantially mute, she enjoyed a wonderful faculty for conversing by signs.
She retained all her teeth, but they were worn low, probably owing to her chewing tough articles of food.

As regards the delicate question of her age, her years, like those of Don Quixote, to all appearance "bordered on the fifties;" but she bowed to all who approached her, and greeted them with a smile. She must have been a Saratoga belle who had retained the sweetness and grace of her manners.
had much better have left her in her brushwood "ranch," on the Island of
, contentedly cross-legged, wearing her simple robe of shag-duck, and supporting existence on a simple diet of cabbage plant, corcomite, and seal blubber.
, and at once won their esteem by the modesty and decorum of her demeanour.
She exhibited singular dexterity in making vessels for holding water out of grass and asphaltum, in both of which
is very prolific; and she also seemed to recognize various appliances of civilization. A teakettle probably, or possibly a fryingpan.
A great future awaited her. Arrived at
, she would have been feted and caressed, and ere long an agent of the ubiquitous
would have arrived on the scene; and
might have been wafted to that Great Temple of Showmanship, the
, there to hold levees in the Halls made famous by the memories of
. Who knows but that, in process of time,
from the Pacific might not have made her appearance in
, and delighted crowded audiences by skinning seal blubber, making water jars from grass and asphalt, and expressing, by a series of animated signs, her opinion on the Eastern Question?
We have been favored with the presence of
the Bearded Lady, Miss Julia Pastrana
would have been, indeed, a rarity. She would have been as popular amongst us as
might have been had he escaped assassination. But this was not to be.
Seven weeks after her arrival of the
sickened and died, partly, it is stated, from the effects of a fall, and partly through an indisposition brought on by eating fruits and vegetables.
She may have pined for her simple menu of "palo santo" and "corcomite," varied by seal blubber; and the circumstance of the fruits and vegetables of
having conduced to her dissolution, must additionally lessen the complacency of the
. A course of lentils might have saved her, but there were none seemingly at hand.
She had been judiciously baptized by
has forwarded the female Crusoe's dress of shag-skins and her baskets and implements to the

It is a pity that she did not recover her speech, if only for a few minutes, before she passed away. She might have told the world whether she was really an Indian squaw, or a Saratoga belle disgusted with and retired from Vanity Fair.—London Telegraph.
